clean_story:
Precision metalwork. Tiny gears for giants.

Philippe Niez’s name hides inside the world’s most famous watches—though you’ll never see it engraved. A master of micromechanics, he designs the invisible: gears thinner than hairs, springs coiled to fit a grain of rice. His workshop in Switzerland’s Jura Mountains supplies parts so exact that Rolex, Patek Philippe, and Audemars Piguet rely on them, yet his company, Horlogerie Niez, employs fewer than 20 people.

The trick? Machines too precise for human hands. Niez’s lathes carve steel at tolerances of 2 microns (a red blood cell is 5). One error—a speck of dust, a tremor—and a 1,000-euro part becomes scrap. His team once spent three months perfecting a 0.3-millimeter screw for a moonphase complication, testing it until it turned smoothly for 50 years’ worth of rotations.

Watchmakers call him when they need the impossible: a gear that won’t wear, a lever that clicks with zero play. Niez’s parts don’t just tell time—they outlast the watches they’re in.

Three related subjects are invisible craftsmanship, Swiss horology secrets, and machines smaller than ants.
